--- SOURCE 4: Robust control of a compliant manipulator with reduced ... ---
URL: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1

SUMMARY:
This paper presents a robust control strategy for compliant manipulators equipped with Discrete Variable Stiffness Actuation (DVSA), aimed at enhancing tracking performance under both fixed and varying stiffness conditions. By compensating for nonlinear dynamics, joint coupling, frictions, stiffness variability and uncertainties through a Sliding Perturbation Observer (SPO), the proposed approach ensures robust and consistent performance, significantly outperforming traditional controllers. Given the inherent flexibility and adaptability of compliant manipulators equipped with Discrete Variable Stiffness Actuators (DVSA), robust control in the joint space is crucial for ensuring precise, robust and reliable performance. Development of a robust and generalized control strategy for DVSA systems: This work introduces a robust PID-SPO control strategy for compliant manipulators with discrete variable stiffness actuation (DVSA). & Lee, M.C. Dynamics analysis and control of 5 dof robot manipulator.

 Robust control of a compliant manipulator with reduced dynamics and sliding perturbation observer 

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1.pdf)

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1.pdf)

*   Article
*   [Open access](https://www.springernature.com/gp/open-research/about/the-fundamentals-of-open-access-and-open-research)
*   Published: 15 March 2025

Robust control of a compliant manipulator with reduced dynamics and sliding perturbation observer
=================================================================================================

*   [Muhammad Salman](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#auth-Muhammad-Salman-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#Aff1), 
*   [Zhenwei Niu](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#auth-Zhenwei-Niu-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#Aff1), 
*   [Rajmeet Singh](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#auth-Rajmeet-Singh-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#Aff1), 
*   [Lochan Kshetrimayum](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#auth-Lochan-Kshetrimayum-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#Aff1)&
*   …
*   [Irfan Hussain](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#auth-Irfan-Hussain-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#Aff1)

Show authors
[_Scientific Reports_](https://www.nature.com/srep)**volume 15**, Article number:8934 (2025) [Cite this article](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#citeas)

*   1442 Accesses

*   [Metrics details](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1/metrics)

Abstract
--------

Compliant manipulators equipped with Variable Stiffness Actuation (VSA) possess the ability to safely interact with their environment and adapt to complex tasks. However, the tracking performance of such manipulators is significantly influenced by their intrinsic compliance and varying stiffness. This paper presents a robust control strategy for compliant manipulators equipped with Discrete Variable Stiffness Actuation (DVSA), aimed at enhancing tracking performance under both fixed and varying stiffness conditions. By compensating for nonlinear dynamics, joint coupling, frictions, stiffness variability and uncertainties through a Sliding Perturbation Observer (SPO), the proposed approach ensures robust and consistent performance, significantly outperforming traditional controllers. In fixed stiffness scenarios, the proposed controller achieves a reduction in energy consumption of up to 68.1% at the lowest stiffness and 75.5% at the highest stiffness, demonstrating superior efficiency. For abrupt stiffness transitions (lowest–highest–lowest), the proposed controller reduces energy consumption by up to 70.9% compared to traditional controllers. Additionally, it maintains smooth control input and precise tracking, avoiding the chattering and inefficiencies that traditional controllers typically exhibit. The SPO effectively estimates all system states and perturbation, compensating for nonlinearities, disturbances due to variable stiffness, and friction, enabling consistent trajectory tracking with minimal control effort. The proposed controller’s ability to adapt to varying stiffness levels without explicit parameter tuning highlights its robustness and practical viability. Simulation and experimental results validate its superior performance in reducing tracking error and energy consumption, particularly in scenarios where traditional controllers struggle to manage varying stiffness effectively.

Introduction
------------

Collaborative robots are now moving beyond traditional industrial settings and are being used in unstructured environments for physical Human–Robot Interaction (pHRI). In these scenarios, compliant manipulators offer significant benefits due to their flexibility, safety, and adaptability, enhancing pHRI[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR1 "Laschi, C., Mazzolai, B. & Cianchetti, M. Soft robotics: Technologies and systems pushing the boundaries of robot abilities. Sci. Robot. 1, eaah3690 (2016)."). Those equipped with variable stiffness actuation (VSA) further improve adaptability and safety by allowing joint stiffness adjustment, which enhances performance during interactions in unstructured environments[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR2 "Yang, S., Chen, P., Wang, D., Yu, Y. & Liu, Y. Design and analysis of a 2-dof actuator with variable stiffness based on leaf springs. J. Bionic Eng. 19, 1392–1404 (2022)."),[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR3 "Ohe, T., Alemayoh, T. T., Lee, J. H. & Okamoto, S. Feedforward operational stiffness modulation and external force estimation of planar robots equipped with variable stiffness actuators. Intel. Serv. Robot. 15, 179–192 (2022)."),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR4 "Awad, M. I. et al. Design of a novel passive binary-controlled variable stiffness joint (bpvsj) towards passive haptic interface application. IEEE Access 6, 63045–63057 (2018)."),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR5 "Hussain, I., Albalasie, A., Awad, M. I., Seneviratne, L. & Gan, D. Modeling, control, and numerical simulations of a novel binary-controlled variable stiffness actuator (bcvsa). Front. Robot. AI 5, 68 (2018)."). Among the various advancements in compliant manipulators, those with Discrete Variable Stiffness Actuators (DVSA) represent a notable innovation. DVSA allows the manipulator’s joints or links to adjust their stiffness dynamically, switching between rigid and flexible states as needed[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR6 "Hussain, I., Albalasie, A., Awad, M. I. & Gan, D. Modeling, identification, and control of a discrete variable stiffness actuator (dvsa). In Actuators, vol. 8, 50 (MDPI, 2019)."),[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR7 "Toubar, H. et al. Design, modeling, and control of a series elastic actuator with discretely adjustable stiffness (seadas). Mechatronics 86, 102863 (2022)."). This capability makes DVSA particularly valuable in soft robotics applications, especially in designing them and for rehabilitation purposes[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR8 "Zhong, Y., Du, R., Guo, P. & Yu, H. Investigation on a new approach for designing articulated soft robots with discrete variable stiffness. IEEE/ASME Trans. Mechatron. 26, 2998–3009 (2021)."),[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR9 "Li, X. et al. Design, modeling and experiments of a variable stiffness soft robotic glove for stroke patients with clenched fist deformity. IEEE Robot. Autom. Lett. 8, 4044–4051 (2023).").The term “discrete” indicates that the stiffness can be set to specific, predefined levels rather than being continuously variable, allowing for controlled and predictable changes in the manipulator’s behaviour. This adaptability offers several advantages across various industrial fields for safe human–robot interaction[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR10 "Fu, J., Yu, Z., Lin, H., Zheng, L. & Gan, D. A novel variable stiffness compliant robotic link based on discrete variable stiffness units for safe human-robot interaction. J. Mech. Robot. 16, 014501 (2024)."). For example, in the event of a collision, the robots can rapidly shift to a safe mode, rendering them physically incapable of causing injury to humans. The use of DVSA in compliant manipulators holds immense potential to enhance safety, productivity, and efficiency in industrial applications.

**Fig. 1**

[![Image 6: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-025-91904-1/MediaObjects/41598_2025_91904_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1/figures/1)

Complaint manipulator having discrete variable stiffness actuation[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR11 "Niu, Z. et al. Towards safe physical human-robot interaction by exploring the rapid stiffness switching feature of discrete variable stiffness actuation. IEEE Robot. Autom. Lett. 7, 8084–8091 (2022).").

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1/figures/1)

In our previous paper[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR11 "Niu, Z. et al. Towards safe physical human-robot interaction by exploring the rapid stiffness switching feature of discrete variable stiffness actuation. IEEE Robot. Autom. Lett. 7, 8084–8091 (2022)."),[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR12 "Hussain, I. et al. Design and control of a discrete variable stiffness actuator with instant stiffness switch for safe human-robot interaction. IEEE Access 9, 118215–118231 (2021)."), we developed a collision detection and reaction pipeline to increase the safety of physical human–robot interaction (pHRI) based on the manipulator equipped with DVSA, as shown in Fig. [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#Fig1). It is combined with a momentum-based detection technique with the rapid stiffness switching feature of the discrete variable stiffness actuation system to properly handle unexpected collisions[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR11 "Niu, Z. et al. Towards safe physical human-robot interaction by exploring the rapid stiffness switching feature of discrete variable stiffness actuation. IEEE Robot. Autom. Lett. 7, 8084–8091 (2022)."). While these studies focused on leveraging the stiffness switching feature for collision safety, the authors did not address the significant challenges introduced by this feature on key performance metrics such as tracking accuracy, control effort, and energy consumption. The rapid stiffness switching in DVSA introduces dynamic variations in the system’s compliance, leading to non-lineaities and disturbances that can adversely affect trajectory tracking precision. In these studies[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR12 "Hussain, I. et al. Design and control of a discrete variable stiffness actuator with instant stiffness switch for safe human-robot interaction. IEEE Access 9, 118215–118231 (2021)."), basic PID and pole-placement techniques were employed for tracking desired trajectories. However, they fail to maintain accuracy during abrupt stiffness transitions and frequently require gain retuning for different stiffness conditions, rendering them impractical for dynamic environments. These variations place additional demands on the control system, often resulting in increased control effort and higher energy consumption. Furthermore, stiffness changes can exacerbate the coupling effects between joints, making it even more challenging to achieve smooth and accurate control. Advanced methods like Gain-Scheduling and Deterministic Robust Control (DRC) provide improved force control by dynamically adjusting gains or leveraging robust models[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR13 "Yu, Z. et al. Dynamic modeling and robust torque control of a discrete variable stiffness actuator. In International Design Engineering Technical Conferences and Computers and Information in Engineering Conference, vol. 87363, V008T08A058 (American Society of Mechanical Engineers, 2023)."). However, these techniques rely on predefined schedules or robust parameter estimation, limiting their ability to handle unforeseen dynamic changes or high-frequency disturbances effectively. Furthermore, these methods lack a focus on energy consumption, a critical requirement for compliant manipulators.

**Fig. 2**

[![Image 7: figure 2](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-025-91904-1/MediaObjects/41598_2025_91904_Fig2_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1/figures/2)

Block diagram of the proposed control algorithm for the compliant manipulator featuring discrete variable stiffness actuation (DVSA).

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1/figures/2)

There are many non-linear controllers which have been tried to date for tracking the desired trajectory. Hwang et al. proposed a nonlinear controller based on the combination of Takagi–Sugeno (T–S) fuzzy system and Sliding Mode Controller (SMC)[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR14 "Hwang, C.-L. A novel Takagi–Sugeno-based robust adaptive fuzzy sliding-mode controller. IEEE Trans. Fuzzy Syst. 12, 676–687 (2004)."). It also reduces the computational burden. Bounemeur et al. present a series of adaptive fuzzy fault-tolerant control approaches for uncertain systems, including SISO and multi-variable nonlinear systems[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR15 "Bounemeur, A., chemachema, M., Zahaf, A. & Bououden, S. Adaptive fuzzy fault-tolerant control using nussbaum gain for a class of siso nonlinear systems with unknown directions. In Proceedings of the 4th International Conference on Electrical Engineering and Control Applications: ICEECA 2019, 17–19 December 2019, Constantine, Algeria, 493–510 (Springer, 2021)."),[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR16 "Bounemeur, A. & Chemachema, M. Adaptive fuzzy fault-tolerant control using Nussbaum-type function with state-dependent actuator failures. Neural Comput. Appl. 33, 191–208 (2021)."), and quadrotor attitude control[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR17 "Bounemeur, A. & Chemachema, M. Optimal adaptive fuzzy fault-tolerant control applied on a quadrotor attitude stabilization based on particle swarm optimization. Proc. Inst. Mech. Eng. I: J. Syst. Control Eng. 238, 704–719 (2024)."), addressing challenges such as unknown control gain signs, actuator faults, external disturbances, and system uncertainties[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR18 "Bey, O. & Chemachema, M. Finite-time event-triggered output-feedback adaptive decentralized echo-state network fault-tolerant control for interconnected pure-feedback nonlinear systems with input saturation and external disturbances: A fuzzy control-error approach. Inf. Sci. 669, 120557 (2024)."),[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR19 "Bey, O. & Chemachema, M. Decentralized event-triggered output feedback adaptive neural network control for a class of mimo uncertain strict-feedback nonlinear systems with input saturation. Int. J. Adapt. Control Signal Process. 38, 1420–1441 (2024)."). Mukherjee et al. introduce the SMC for the planer snake robot to ensure stability while considering uncertainties[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR20 "Mukherjee, J., Roy, S., Kar, I. N. & Mukherjee, S. Maneuvering control of planar snake robot: An adaptive robust approach with artificial time delay. Int. J. Robust Nonlinear Control 31, 3982–3999 (2021)."). In recent years, much research has focused on AI-based controllers. Pane et al. proposed two types of Reinforcement Learning (RL) based compensation models for better control performance[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR21 "Pane, Y. P., Nageshrao, S. P., Kober, J. & Babuška, R. Reinforcement learning based compensation methods for robot manipulators. Eng. Appl. Artif. Intell. 78, 236–247 (2019)."). The different kinds of reference paths such as square and circle are used to evaluate the performance. Cao et al. solved the problem of controlling the robot manipulator system with unknown disturbance by combining NTSM control with RL[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR22 "Cao, S., Sun, L., Jiang, J. & Zuo, Z. Reinforcement learning-based fixed-time trajectory tracking control for uncertain robotic manipulators with input saturation. IEEE Trans. Neural Netw. Learn. Syst. 34, 4584–4595 (2021)."). Sliding Mode Controller has chattering issues as the gain increases[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR23 "Bartolini, G., Ferrara, A. & Usai, E. Chattering avoidance by second-order sliding mode control. IEEE Trans. Autom. Control 43, 241–246 (1998)."),[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR24 "Edwards, C. & Spurgeon, S. Sliding Mode Control: Theory and Applications (CRC Press, London, 1998).") whereas RL methods are very high computing and time-consuming methods[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR25 "Nguyen, T. T., Nguyen, N. D. & Nahavandi, S. Deep reinforcement learning for multiagent systems: A review of challenges, solutions, and applications. IEEE Trans. Cybernet. 50, 3826–3839 (2020).") and practical implementation of these algorithms are still in research discussion. By effectively handling uncertainties and disturbances, robust controllers can optimize the control effort, leading to more efficient use of actuators and minimizing unnecessary energy expenditure. Scaler et al. proposed an energy-efficient method for the optimal design of elastic elements[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR26 "Scalera, L. et al. Energy efficiency in a 4-dof parallel robot featuring compliant elements. Int. J. Mech. Control 20, 49–57 (2019)."). Arakelian et al. came up with the solution of input torque minimization of two degrees of freedom serial manipulators based on minimum energy control and optimal redistribution of movable masses[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR27 "Arakelian, V., Le Baron, J.-P. & Mottu, P. Torque minimisation of the 2-dof serial manipulators based on minimum energy consideration and optimum mass redistribution. Mechatronics 21, 310–314 (2011)."). Another study from the literature, introduces an improved energy production strategy using PID[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR28 "Bounemeur, A., Zahaf, A. & Chemachema, M. Pid controller for precise voltage output of solid oxide fuel cell. In International Conference of Nanotechnology for Environmental Protection and Clean Energy Production, 319–326 (Springer, 2023).") model predictive control with an event-triggered mechanism[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR29 "Zahaf, A., Bounemeur, A. & Bououden, S. Wind turbine energy production based on event-triggered speed control. In International Conference of Nanotechnology for Environmental Protection and Clean Energy Production, 311–318 (Springer, 2023).").

Given the inherent flexibility and adaptability of compliant manipulators equipped with Discrete Variable Stiffness Actuators (DVSA), robust control in the joint space is crucial for ensuring precise, robust and reliable performance. The ability of a DVSA-based manipulator to dynamically adjust stiffness at discrete levels requires a control strategy capable of handling rapid stiffness changes and energy efficient while maintaining precision and accuracy. However, controllers specifically designed for this unique system are lacking in the current workspace. The need for robust control is driven by challenges such as variable stiffness, nonlinearities, and disturbances[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR30 "De Luca, A., Albu-Schaffer, A., Haddadin, S. & Hirzinger, G. Collision detection and safe reaction with the dlr-iii lightweight manipulator arm. In 2006 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems, 1623–1630 (IEEE, 2006)."),[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR31 "Spong, M. W., Hutchinson, S. & Vidyasagar, M. Robot Modeling and Control (Wiley, 2020)."). Additionally, noise in measuring velocity, torque, and dynamic uncertainty makes traditional controllers inadequate for achieving consistent and accurate trajectory tracking[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR11 "Niu, Z. et al. Towards safe physical human-robot interaction by exploring the rapid stiffness switching feature of discrete variable stiffness actuation. IEEE Robot. Autom. Lett. 7, 8084–8091 (2022)."),[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR32 "Abbasi, S. J., Khan, H., Lee, J. W., Salman, M. & Lee, M. C. Robust control design for accurate trajectory tracking of multi-degree-of-freedom robot manipulator in virtual simulator. IEEE Access 10, 17155–17168 (2022)."). To ensure robust controller performance, understanding the manipulator’s dynamics is essential[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR33 "Salman, M., Khan, H., Abbasi, S. J. & Lee, M. C. Dynamics analysis and control of 5 dof robot manipulator. In 2021 21st International Conference on Control, Automation and Systems (ICCAS), 1705–1709 (IEEE, 2021)."). A simpler system dynamic can enhance control performance[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR34 "Franklin, G. F. et al. Feedback Control of Dynamic Systems Vol. 3 (Addison-Wesley, 1994)."). Simplifying the dynamics enhances system predictability and modelling, thereby leading to more effective control strategies[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR35 "Doyle, J., Glover, K., Khargonekar, P. & Francis, B. State-space solutions to standard h 2 and h infty control problems. In 1988 American Control Conference, 1691–1696 (IEEE, 1988).").

To address these challenges, this work introduces a novel robust control strategy tailored for compliant manipulators equipped with DVSA. By integrating reduced dynamics modeling with a Sliding Perturbation Observer (SPO)[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR36 "Moura, J. T., Elmali, H. & Olgac, N. Sliding mode control with sliding perturbation observer. J. Dyn. Syst. Meas. Control 119, 657–665 (1997)."),[37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#ref-CR37 "Kim, H. H., Lee, M. C., Kyung, J. H. & Do, H. M. Evaluation of force estimation method based on sliding perturbation observer for dual-arm robot system. Int. J. Control Autom. Syst. 19, 1–10 (2021)."), the controller systematically compensates for nonlinearities, joint coupling, and stiffness variability inherent in DVSA systems. Unlike conventional approaches, the proposed method eliminates the necessity for frequent gain retuning, ensuring robust and consistent performance across diverse stiffness conditions. This significantly reduces manual intervention while enhancing the system’s adaptability and precision. Additionally, the controller exhibits superior energy efficiency, making it a highly practical and effective solution for dynamic and safety-critical robotic applications. Figure [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91904-1#Fig2) describe the framework of the proposed controller

### Main contribution

The main contributions of this study is presented as follows:

*   **Development of a robust and generalized control strategy for DVSA systems**: This work introduces a robust PID-SPO control strategy for compliant manipulators with discrete variable stiffness actuation (DVSA). By addressing challenges such as nonlinear dynamics, joint coupling, and stiffness variability, the proposed controller ensures accurate disturbance compensation through reduced dynamics and the Sliding Perturbation Observer (SPO). It achieves consistent performance across low, high, and dynamically changing stiffness levels, requiring PID gains to be tuned only once for high stiffness. This adaptability makes it highly suitable for real-world applications, including physical human–robot interaction and safety-critical tasks.

*   **Energy-efficient control with superior tracking accuracy**: In fixed stiffness scenarios, the proposed controller demonstrates remarkable energy efficiency, achieving reductions of up to 68.1% at the lowest stiffness and 75.5% at the highest stiffness compared to traditional controllers. Additionally, under dynamically changing stiffness condition

enriched_points.expanded_content: Robust control strategies enhance trajectory tracking accuracy in industrial manipulators, addressing uncertainties and nonlinearities, ensuring reliable performance in dynamic manufacturing environments.

Robust controllers enable manipulators to manage varying loads effectively, maintaining stability and performance across diverse operational conditions, thereby improving efficiency in industrial applications.
Numerous case studies demonstrate robust control applications in manufacturing, showcasing improved precision, efficiency, and adaptability in robotic systems amidst varying operational challenges.
